summary: The article reports on new Chinese export controls on rare earth materials and technologies, specifically targeting those used in advanced chipmaking (14nm logic chips and 256-layer memory) and military AI. An industry observer, Xiang Ligang, suggests these controls indicate that China has largely overcome its semiconductor equipment bottlenecks and is shifting from a defensive to an offensive posture in the 'chip war' with the US.
main_claims: China has implemented new export controls on rare earths used in advanced chipmaking and military AI.
Industry observer Xiang Ligang claims China has resolved its semiconductor equipment bottlenecks and developed world-class tools.
China is shifting from a defensive to an offensive strategy in the global chip rivalry.
China may expand export controls to chip-making equipment in the future.
evidence_or_facts: New export control rules take effect December 1, 2025.
Case-by-case review for exports of rare earths for logic chips $\le$ 14nm and memory chips $\ge$ 256 layers.
Case-by-case review for AI with potential military uses.
China produces ~70% of mined rare earth output and ~80% of refined materials globally.
Neodymium and dysprosium are used in lithography machine motors; lanthanum and cerium are used in optical glass and polishing compounds.
Separate export controls announced on lithium battery and graphite anode materials.
rhetorical_frames: The 'chip war' as a strategic competition for technological dominance.
Shift from 'defense to offense' in geopolitical rivalry.
state_and_policy_content: Ministry of Commerce and Customs announced export controls on rare earths, lithium battery materials, and graphite anode materials.
Implementation of a 'case-by-case review' mechanism for specific high-tech end-uses.
Use of trade policy as a countermeasure to US restrictions on advanced chips and AI processors.
capital_accumulation_content: Mention of Chinese companies reaching world-class levels of equipment development in a few years.
Focus on moving from 'mature to advanced processes' in chip manufacturing.
Expansion of production capacity and strengthening of manufacturing as the next strategic step.
infrastructure_and_supply_chain_content: China's dominance in the rare earth supply chain (mining and refining).
The critical role of rare earths as indirect but essential inputs for lithography machines and other semiconductor equipment.
The vulnerability of foreign makers of advanced lithography systems to Chinese rare earth supplies.
geopolitical_competition_content: Ongoing struggle for technological dominance between Beijing and Washington.
US curbs on China's access to advanced chips and manufacturing tools since 2019.
China's use of critical mineral controls as a strategic lever against US-led restrictions.
possible_theory_hooks: Strategic trade policy and the use of resource monopolies to counter technological sanctions.
The relationship between R&D breakthroughs (overcoming bottlenecks) and the shift in trade policy posture.
The role of critical mineral supply chains as a 'choke point' in high-tech industrial policy.
The interaction between state-led industrial policy and the speed of domestic semiconductor equipment development.
what_is_missing_for_theory_evaluation: Verification of Xiang Ligang's claims regarding the resolution of equipment bottlenecks (is this an observer's opinion or a verified fact?).
Detailed data on the amount of actual export disruptions caused by these controls.
Detailed financial data on the capital expenditure of the Chinese state or firms to achieve these breakthroughs.
Specifics on the how the 'case-by-case review' is actually applied in practice (discretionary vs. rule-based).
